name: GPD Physics Benchmark — Classical Mechanics
description: Benchmark tasks from classical mechanics: Lagrangian/Hamiltonian dynamics, symmetry, conservation laws, and nonlinear dynamics.

tasks.title: Kepler problem via Hamilton-Jacobi
tasks.description: Solve the Kepler problem using the Hamilton-Jacobi method to derive the orbit equation.
tasks.problem_statement: For a particle of mass m in a gravitational potential V(r) = -GMm/r, use the Hamilton-Jacobi equation to derive the orbit equation r(phi). Verify that the result gives a conic section.
given: Hamilton-Jacobi equation: H(q, partial S/partial q) + partial S/partial t = 0
assumptions: Two-body problem reduced to one body in center-of-mass frame
Planar motion (conservation of angular momentum vector)
tasks.expected_answer: r(phi) = L^2/(GMm^2) * 1/(1 + e cos(phi - phi_0)) where e = sqrt(1 + 2EL^2/(G^2 M^2 m^3)) is the eccentricity.
verification_hints: For E < 0: e < 1 (ellipse), orbit is bound
The semi-latus rectum p = L^2/(GMm^2) and semi-major axis a = -GMm/(2E) for ellipses
Check: perihelion distance r_min = a(1-e), aphelion r_max = a(1+e)

tasks.title: Normal modes of a double pendulum (small oscillations)
tasks.description: Find the normal mode frequencies of a double pendulum in the small-angle approximation.
tasks.problem_statement: A double pendulum consists of two identical masses m connected by two identical massless rods of length l. Using the Lagrangian method with small-angle approximation, find the two normal mode frequencies.
given: Two identical rods of length l
Small angle approximation: sin(theta) ~ theta, cos(theta) ~ 1 - theta^2/2
assumptions: Small oscillations about the equilibrium (both angles small)
conventions: theta_1 = angle of upper rod from vertical
theta_2 = angle of lower rod from vertical
tasks.expected_answer: omega_1^2 = g/l * (2 - sqrt(2)), omega_2^2 = g/l * (2 + sqrt(2))
verification_hints: In the slow mode, both pendulums swing in phase; in the fast mode, they swing out of phase
For a single pendulum: omega^2 = g/l, which should be recoverable in appropriate limits

tasks.title: Dimensions of the gravitational constant
tasks.description: Determine the dimensions of Newton's gravitational constant G from the law of gravitation and verify using Kepler's third law.
tasks.problem_statement: From Newton's law of gravitation F = GMm/r^2, determine the SI dimensions of G. Then verify this by requiring Kepler's third law T^2 = (4 pi^2 / (GM)) a^3 to be dimensionally consistent.
given: Newton's law: F = G M m / r^2
Kepler's third law: T^2 = (4 pi^2) / (G M) * a^3
SI dimensions: [F] = kg m s^{-2}, [M] = [m] = kg, [r] = m, [T] = s, [a] = m
assumptions: Point masses or spherically symmetric bodies
verification_hints: In Planck units, G = 1 by definition
